title: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent
concept: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent updates weights using a small group of random examples at once, balancing speed and stability.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) over standard Batch Gradient Descent?

options.text: It updates weights much faster because it only checks one random example at a time.
It always follows a perfectly straight path to the bottom.
It does not require any training data to learn.

exercises.explain: Correct! SGD is lightning fast because it doesn't wait to check the whole dataset. But can we make it less chaotic?

cards.title: The goldilocks choice
cards.body: Reading all 1 million rows of data is too slow. Reading just 1 row at a time is too erratic. Why not read a small, manageable pack of rows instead?

cards.title: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent
cards.body: Mini-Batch Gradient Descent splits the dataset into small, random groups called batches (typically 32, 64, or 128 rows) to update weights.

cards.title: Shopping in batches
cards.body: If you're stocking a pantry, you don't buy 1 can of soup per trip (SGD), nor do you wait to buy 5,000 cans at once (Batch GD). You buy a grocery bag of 32 cans.

cards.title: GPU superpower
cards.body: Modern computer chips (GPUs) can process small groups of numbers in parallel. Processing a batch of 64 rows takes almost the same time as processing just 1!

cards.title: Size does matter
cards.body: Batch size is a crucial setting. A batch too small (like 2) behaves like noisy SGD. A batch too large (like 10,000) wastes memory and slows updates.

cards.title: Comparing batch sizes
cards.body: How the size of the batch affects training speed and path quality:

left.text:
Lightning fast updates
Path is highly chaotic

right.text:
GPU-friendly speed
Path is smooth and steady

exercises.prompt: What is a 'mini-batch' in gradient descent?

options.text: A small, randomly selected subset of the training data.
A smaller version of the full model.
A single pixel in an image dataset.
A tool for deleting old weights.

exercises.explain: Yes! A mini-batch is a small group of data points (like 32 or 64) processed together.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Modern GPUs are designed to process mini-batches in parallel, making them highly efficient.
exercises.explain: True! GPUs excel at matrix operations, meaning they can process 32 or 64 rows simultaneously in almost the same time as a single row.

exercises.sentence: A common mini-batch size used in training models is ___ examples.
exercises.explain: Powers of two like 32, 64, or 128 are standard mini-batch sizes because they fit neatly into GPU memory hardware.

exercises.prompt: What happens if your mini-batch size is set to the size of your entire dataset?

options.text: It behaves exactly like standard Batch Gradient Descent.
It behaves exactly like Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD).
The training will run infinitely fast.
The model will run out of memory instantly.

exercises.explain: Spot on! If the batch is the entire dataset, you are back to standard Batch GD, losing the speed advantage.

exercises.prompt: You are training a model on 100,000 reviews. Standard GD takes 10 minutes per step. SGD is too noisy. You configure a batch size of 64. What is the result?

options.text: You get fast weight updates leveraging parallel GPU hardware, with a path that is much smoother than SGD.
The model will train slower than standard GD because of the extra partitions.
The accuracy will drop to zero because 64 is too small of a number.
The model will only learn from the first 64 reviews and ignore the rest.

exercises.explain: Fantastic! Mini-batch GD is the industry standard because it blends speed, GPU parallelization, and path stability. Next: how to adjust step sizes dynamically as we learn!
